Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore in 1998 as part of a group of related companies in the marine services industry known as the Mectrade Group.
Before 14 October 2011, the shares in Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd were held by Foo Sack You and Foo Siew Wei in the ratio of 55:45 respectively.
In or around early 2012, the shares in the Mectrade Group companies that had been held by Foo Sack You devolved to his widow, Chen Hong Hua, and their two children, Foo Su Mian and Foo Yong Yee.
From early 2012, the shareholders of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd were Chen Hong Hua, Foo Su Mian and Foo Yong Yee.
Around 1975, Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd was incorporated and became part of the Mectrade Group operated by Foo Sack You and Foo Tak Yi.

Up to 14 October 2011, the Mectrade Group comprised several companies including Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd, Mectrade Fabricators Pte Ltd, Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd, Sen-Hong Piping and Engineering Pte Ltd, Mec-Sin Precision Engineering Services, and Mec-Con Industries Pte Ltd.

As at 14 October 2011, the shareholdings in the Mectrade Group companies were: Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd held 55% by Foo Sack You and 45% by Foo Siew Wei; Mectrade Fabricators Pte Ltd held 50% by Foo Sack You and 50% by Foo Tak Yi; Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd held 50% by Foo Sack You and 50% by Foo Tak Yi; Sen-Hong Piping and Engineering Pte Ltd held 55% by Foo Sack You, 22.5% by Lee Hui, and 22.5% by the wife of Foo Tak Yi; Mec-Sin Precision Engineering Services was a sole proprietorship owned by Foo Sack You; Mec-Con Industries Pte Ltd was wholly owned by the family of the defendants.
Between 2007 and 2014, Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd acted as a resident contractor of Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd at Dyna-Mac's shipyard at 45 Gul Road, Singapore 629350.
From 2007 to 2014, Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd undertook mainly structural works such as steel fabrication for Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd as a resident contractor.
During the period 2007 to 2014, Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd operated a shipyard with about 40 to 50 resident contractors, including Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd.

The companies in the Mectrade Group, including Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd, shared common resources and engaged in inter-company transactions and inter-company billings over a period of about 30 years.

Until 14 October 2011, the business of the Mectrade Group was operated by Foo Sack You and Foo Tak Yi, with assistance from family members including Foo Siew Wei and Foo Siew Ping in finance and accounting matters.
Up to 14 October 2011, the registered directors of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd were Foo Sack You and Foo Siew Wei.
Before 14 October 2011, Foo Sack You mainly managed administrative and accounting matters for the Mectrade Group from the office, while Foo Tak Yi managed operations at shipyards.
Before 14 October 2011, Foo Siew Wei and Foo Siew Ping worked in the office and assisted with finance and accounting matters for the Mectrade Group.
Before 14 October 2011, Chen Hong Hua worked as personal assistant to her husband, Foo Sack You, and was rarely present in the office of the Mectrade Group companies.
On 14 October 2011, Foo Sack You passed away.
After the death of Foo Sack You and by early 2012, Foo Siew Ping was appointed as a director of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd following consultation between her, Foo Siew Wei and Foo Tak Yi.
From early 2012, the shareholders of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd did not receive dividends or salary from the company according to their account.
From 2011 to 2018, the shareholders of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd did not receive the company's accounts until they were provided in 2018.
On or about 2 March 2016, a settlement agreement was entered into between Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd, Mectrade Fabricators Pte Ltd, Foo Siew Wei, Foo Tak Yi and Chen Hong Hua.

Under the settlement agreement dated around 2 March 2016, the 45% shareholding in Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd held by Foo Siew Wei was transferred to Chen Hong Hua without contractual provision for payment for those shares.

Under the settlement agreement dated around 2 March 2016, Foo Siew Wei and Foo Siew Ping paid a sum of $1,600,000 to Chen Hong Hua for her shares in Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd, and those shares were transferred.
In 2017, Suit 667 of 2017 was commenced by the defendants against Chen Hong Hua seeking specific performance of obligations under the settlement agreement.
On 22 July 2019, Suit 667 of 2017 was discontinued.

On 12 September 2014, an extraordinary general meeting of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd was held in the absence of Foo Siew Wei and Foo Siew Ping, at which two resolutions were passed to remove them as directors.
Notice of the extraordinary general meeting of 12 September 2014 was stated to have been sent to Foo Siew Wei and Foo Siew Ping, but the notice document was not produced in this suit.

On 1 December 2014, the Ministry of Manpower notified Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd that the work permits of its foreign workers had been revoked due to non-payment of foreign worker levy for September and October 2014.

On 16 December 2014, Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd issued a cheque payment of $38,332.67 to Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd for foreign worker levy, as recorded in a Dyna-Mac payment voucher.

On 17 December 2014, Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd issued a cheque payment of $12,740 to Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd for the cost of return air tickets for repatriating foreign workers, as recorded in a payment voucher.

In 2014, Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd issued a cheque for $32,386.50 to Foo Siew Wei for the purpose of paying salaries of foreign workers engaged under Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd.
Up to late 2014, Foo Tak Yi was involved in the day-to-day management and operations of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd.
Up to around 2014, all projects undertaken by Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd for Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd were supervised by Foo Tak Yi.

Between 2005 and 2013, Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd received substantial revenue from Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd and Mectrade Fabricators Pte Ltd for labour supply under inter-company arrangements.
In October 2014, bank account statements of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd recorded an entry labelled 'BFWL' for $13,500, which referred to foreign workers' levy.
By 2014, the volume of work from Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd to Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd had decreased compared to earlier years.
Around 2014, the work of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd for Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd concluded, broadly coinciding with the removal of Foo Siew Wei and Foo Siew Ping as directors.

From around early 2012, disputes arose between the shareholders of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd and the defendants, leading to several lawsuits and culminating in the 2016 settlement agreement.
On 24 December 2018, the defendants served a List of Documents in this suit which included accounting and business records of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd.
On 17 July 2019, further documents of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd were disclosed pursuant to specific discovery requests.
On 19 July 2019, additional documents of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd were disclosed pursuant to specific discovery requests.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and inter-company dealings within the Mectrade Group, the directors and related individuals caused financial loss or loss of business value to Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd through their management of labour resources, handling of foreign worker-related payments, and allocation of projects and revenues.
Party_Details.Rule: Company directors and persons effectively managing a company are generally manage the company’s affairs in the company’s interests, including proper handling of company funds, maintaining adequate records, and managing resources and business opportunities in a way that does not improperly disadvantage the company.
Party_Details.Application: Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd operated within a group structure where resources and labour were shared and inter-company billings occurred over many years. The company relied heavily on Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd as a primary customer and on labour arrangements that required foreign worker sponsorship and levy payments. After the death of Foo Sack You, shareholdings shifted to his family members while operational control and project supervision remained with the defendants. Inter-company labour supply and revenue flows continued, and foreign worker-related payments were made through Dyna-Mac and the company. The shareholders later obtained access to accounts and records and reviewed historical transactions and workforce arrangements to assess whether these patterns of conduct corresponded with financial loss or reduction in the value of the business.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court analysed extensive allegations by the plaintiff that the defendants had engaged in conflicts of interest, misappropriated monies, mismanaged labour resources, diverted business opportunities, failed to keep proper records, and wrongfully included persons on the payroll, both before and after 24 September 2014. The judge held that merely holding multiple directorships within the Mectrade Group and sharing resources did not in itself create a conflict of interest, particularly given the limited overlap in business activities and the resident contractor structure that constrained project allocation. On alleged misappropriations, the court found that specific sums relating to foreign worker levies, salaries, and repatriation costs were in fact paid and accounted for, and that many other monetary claims were unparticularised, unsupported by evidence, or later conceded or abandoned. The court emphasised that the plaintiff bore the burden of proving both liability and loss and that broad references to equitable compensation principles did not relieve it of this burden.

Regarding labour management, the court accepted evidence from Dyna-Mac’s representative and the defence expert that the marine industry’s project flow was uneven and that maintaining a stable foreign workforce was commercially rational. Redeployment of underutilised workers to other Mectrade Group companies under labour supply arrangements, at rates originally set by Foo Sack You, was found to be a continuation of existing practice rather than a diversion of free labour. The plaintiff failed to show that any specific projects were offered to it and then diverted, or to quantify any lost profits. Claims that records were inaccurate or exaggerated were not substantiated, and hearsay assertions about an accountant allegedly being asked to do illegal things were rejected.

For events after 24 September 2014, the court held that once the first and second defendants were removed as directors, they no longer owed fiduciary duties to the plaintiff, and the plaintiff had not pleaded or proved an alternative tortious cause of action. The repatriation of foreign workers followed non-payment of levies by the company under the new shareholders’ control and revocation of work permits by the Ministry of Manpower; the court found that the defendants’ actions in coordinating repatriation and payments with Dyna-Mac were responsive to this situation and not a deliberate destruction of the business. The court also found that the real cause of the plaintiff’s subsequent business difficulties lay in the shareholders’ lack of familiarity with the business and the loss of the operational relationship with Dyna-Mac and the wider group, rather than in any failure to hand over documents or chattels.

As to the third defendant, the pleadings did not clearly articulate a cause of action, and the plaintiff expressly stated that he was not a director or officer of the plaintiff while vaguely alleging a breach of duty. The court rejected a late attempt in closing submissions to characterise him as a director within the meaning of the Companies Act or as a shadow director, noting that this was neither pleaded nor put to him in cross-examination and was inconsistent with the pleaded assertion that he had no authority to deal with the company’s affairs. Even if fiduciary duties had applied, the court considered that the factual findings made in relation to the first and second defendants would have led to the same conclusion of no actionable breach or proven loss. Consequently, the court dismissed all of the plaintiff’s claims against all defendants and invited submissions on costs.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Foo Siew Wei

Facts.Text: As at 14 October 2011, Foo Siew Wei held 45% of the shares in Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd, with the remaining 55% held by Foo Sack You.
Up to 14 October 2011, Foo Siew Wei was a registered director of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd together with Foo Sack You.
Before 14 October 2011, Foo Siew Wei worked in the office of the Mectrade Group and assisted with finance and accounting matters.

After the death of Foo Sack You and by early 2012, Foo Siew Wei remained a director of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd and invited her sister, Foo Siew Ping, to assume the role of director after consulting her and their father, Foo Tak Yi.

After early 2012, Foo Siew Wei did not consider it natural for members of Foo Sack You’s family to succeed him as director of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd because they had not been working in the company.
On 12 September 2014, an extraordinary general meeting of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd was held in the absence of Foo Siew Wei, at which a resolution was passed to remove her as a director.
Notice of the extraordinary general meeting on 12 September 2014 was stated to have been sent to Foo Siew Wei, but the notice document was not produced in this suit.

In 2014, Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd issued a cheque for $32,386.50 to Foo Siew Wei for the purpose of paying salaries of foreign workers engaged under Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd.
In late 2014, Foo Siew Wei liaised with Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd regarding payment of foreign workers' salaries and arrangements for repatriation after work permits were revoked.

On or about 2 March 2016, under a settlement agreement, the 45% shareholding in Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd held by Foo Siew Wei was transferred to Chen Hong Hua without contractual provision for payment for those shares.
On or about 2 March 2016, under the settlement agreement, Foo Siew Wei and Foo Siew Ping paid a sum of $1,600,000 to Chen Hong Hua for her shares in Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute management decisions and inter-company dealings undertaken by Foo Siew Wei in her capacity as director and shareholder of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd, including handling of foreign worker-related funds and participation in group labour and revenue arrangements, corresponded with any ultimate finding of liability or non-liability.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals acting as company directors are generally assessed against standards requiring them to manage company affairs and funds in the company’s interests, including proper handling of payments, participation in group arrangements, and responses to regulatory and operational constraints.
Party_Details.Application: As a director and 45% shareholder of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd until her removal by resolution in September 2014, Foo Siew Wei participated in finance and accounting functions within the Mectrade Group and in arrangements with Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd. She received a cheque from Dyna-Mac in 2014 to pay foreign workers’ salaries and later coordinated with Dyna-Mac on salary and repatriation matters after work permits were revoked. Her shareholding in the plaintiff was later transferred to Chen Hong Hua under a settlement agreement, while she and her sister made a substantial payment for shares in another group company. These pre-dispute actions formed the factual basis on which the court evaluated whether her conduct met or fell short of the applicable standards for directors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court held that the plaintiff failed to prove that Foo Siew Wei engaged in actionable misconduct. It rejected the contention that her concurrent roles in multiple Mectrade Group companies created an inherent conflict of interest, noting the limited overlap in business activities and the structural constraints imposed by the resident contractor system. Allegations that she misappropriated specific sums relating to foreign worker levies, salaries, and repatriation costs were examined in detail; documentary evidence showed that the levy and repatriation sums were paid by Dyna-Mac to the plaintiff, and the salary cheque issued to her was used for its intended purpose, as corroborated by Dyna-Mac’s representative and the absence of complaints from the Ministry of Manpower. Other monetary claims were unparticularised, unsupported, or later conceded.

On labour management, the court accepted that maintaining a relatively stable foreign workforce was commercially rational in a volatile project environment and that redeployment of underutilised workers to other group companies under labour supply agreements, at rates originally set by Foo Sack You, was a continuation of established practice rather than a diversion of free labour. The plaintiff did not prove that any specific projects were offered to it and then diverted, nor did it quantify any lost profits. Claims that accounting records were inaccurate or exaggerated were not substantiated, and hearsay assertions about an accountant being asked to do illegal things were rejected. For events after her removal as director, the court found that she no longer owed fiduciary duties and that the plaintiff had not pleaded or proved an alternative cause of action. Overall, the court concluded that the evidence did not establish that Foo Siew Wei was liable on any of the pleaded bases.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Foo Siew Ping

Facts.Text: After the death of Foo Sack You and by early 2012, Foo Siew Ping was appointed as a director of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd following an invitation from her sister, Foo Siew Wei, after consultation with their father, Foo Tak Yi.
From early 2012 until her removal in 2014, Foo Siew Ping worked in the office of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd and assisted with finance and accounting matters.
On 12 September 2014, an extraordinary general meeting of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd was held in the absence of Foo Siew Ping, at which a resolution was passed to remove her as a director.
Notice of the extraordinary general meeting on 12 September 2014 was stated to have been sent to Foo Siew Ping, but the notice document was not produced in this suit.
On or about 2 March 2016, under a settlement agreement, Foo Siew Ping and Foo Siew Wei paid a sum of $1,600,000 to Chen Hong Hua for her shares in Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute management and inter-company dealings undertaken by Foo Siew Ping in her capacity as director of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd corresponded with any ultimate finding of liability or non-liability.
Party_Details.Rule: Company directors are generally assessed against standards requiring them to manage company affairs and resources in the company’s interests, including participation in group arrangements and handling of company-related finances.
Party_Details.Application: From early 2012 until her removal by resolution in September 2014, Foo Siew Ping served as a director of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd and assisted with finance and accounting functions within the Mectrade Group. She later participated, together with her sister, in a settlement agreement under which they paid a substantial sum for shares in another group company. These pre-dispute actions formed part of the factual matrix considered by the court in determining whether her conduct met the applicable standards for directors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered the allegations against Foo Siew Ping together with those against her sister, as they were both directors during the relevant period. It found that the plaintiff had not proved that she engaged in any actionable misconduct. The court rejected the argument that her concurrent directorships within the Mectrade Group created an inherent conflict of interest, given the limited overlap in business activities and the structural constraints of the resident contractor system. Allegations of misappropriation, mismanagement of labour, diversion of business opportunities, and improper record-keeping were not supported by specific evidence linking her to any wrongful handling of funds or resources. Many of the monetary claims were unparticularised or unsupported, and the plaintiff did not demonstrate that any specific projects were diverted from the plaintiff or quantify any resulting loss. For events after her removal as director, the court held that she no longer owed fiduciary duties and that no alternative cause of action had been pleaded or proved. Accordingly, the court concluded that Foo Siew Ping was not liable on the pleaded claims.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Foo Tak Yi

Facts.Text: Around 1975, Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd was incorporated and became part of the Mectrade Group operated by Foo Sack You and Foo Tak Yi, who entered into business together and grew their business as close friends.
As at 14 October 2011, the shares in Mectrade Fabricators Pte Ltd and Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd were held 50% by Foo Sack You and 50% by Foo Tak Yi.
As at 14 October 2011, the shares in Sen-Hong Piping and Engineering Pte Ltd were held 55% by Foo Sack You, 22.5% by Lee Hui, and 22.5% by the wife of Foo Tak Yi.
Up to 14 October 2011, Foo Tak Yi was in charge of operations for the Mectrade Group, working on the ground at shipyards, while administrative and accounting matters were handled by Foo Sack You.
Until late 2014, Foo Tak Yi was involved in the day-to-day management and operations of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd.
Between 2007 and 2014, all projects undertaken by Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd for Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd were supervised by Foo Tak Yi.

From 2007 to 2014, Foo Tak Yi and the owner of Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd had a good personal and professional relationship, which facilitated the securing of several projects for Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd.

After the Ministry of Manpower revoked the work permits of Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd's foreign workers on 1 December 2014 due to unpaid levies, Foo Tak Yi liaised with Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd to arrange for payment of outstanding salaries and repatriation of the workers.
In 2014, when contacted by Foo Tak Yi about foreign worker issues, Chen Hong Hua responded that she had nothing to do with the matter, according to his account.
In 2014, Foo Tak Yi redeployed underutilised foreign workers sponsored for Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd to projects of other Mectrade Group companies so that their salaries could be paid.

On or about 2 March 2016, Foo Tak Yi was a party to a settlement agreement with Mectrade Engineering (Pte) Ltd, Mectrade Fabricators Pte Ltd, Foo Siew Wei and Chen Hong Hua concerning share transfers and payments within the Mectrade Group.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute operational and management conduct of Foo Tak Yi in relation to Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd and the wider Mectrade Group corresponded with any ultimate finding of liability or non-liability, given his role in supervising projects, managing foreign workers, and participating in group arrangements.
Party_Details.Rule: Persons who effectively manage or influence a company’s operations may, depending on their role and legal characterisation, be assessed against standards similar to those applied to directors, including expectations regarding management of projects, workforce, and company-related funds.
Party_Details.Application: Over several decades, Foo Tak Yi co-operated with Foo Sack You in building the Mectrade Group, holding equal shareholdings in key group companies and managing operations at shipyards. Between 2007 and 2014, he supervised all projects undertaken by Hamid Marine Services & Engrg Pte Ltd for Dyna-Mac Engineering Services Pte Ltd and maintained a strong relationship with Dyna-Mac’s owner, which facilitated project awards. He redeployed underutilised foreign workers sponsored for the plaintiff to other group projects under labour supply arrangements and later coordinated with Dyna-Mac on salary payments and repatriation after work permits were revoked due to unpaid levies. He also joined in a 2016 settlement agreement concerning share transfers and payments within the group. These actions formed the factual basis on which the court considered whether any legal responsibility attached to him for the plaintiff’s losses.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court found that the plaintiff had not clearly articulated or pleaded a coherent cause of action against Foo Tak Yi. The Statement of Claim expressly stated that he was not a director or officer of the plaintiff and vaguely alleged a breach of duty without specifying its legal basis. During trial, counsel described his role as having 'acted against the company' by assisting the two directors, but did not identify a recognised cause of action. A later attempt in closing submissions to characterise him as a director within the meaning of the Companies Act, or as a shadow director, was rejected because it was not pleaded, was not put to him in cross-examination, and was inconsistent with the pleaded assertion that he had no authority to deal with the plaintiff’s affairs.

Substantively, the court observed that even if fiduciary duties had applied, the factual findings made in relation to the first and second defendants would have led to the same outcome for Foo Tak Yi. The court accepted that he supervised projects for Dyna-Mac, managed operations, and redeployed underutilised foreign workers to other group companies under labour supply arrangements originally instituted by Foo Sack You. It found that these practices were commercially rational in the context of the industry and did not amount to diversion of free labour or misappropriation. His role in coordinating salary payments and repatriation after the Ministry of Manpower revoked work permits was seen as a response to the company’s non-payment of levies under the new shareholders’ control, rather than as an act causing the plaintiff’s loss. The court therefore concluded that no actionable breach or proven loss was established against him and dismissed the claims.
